title: Stochastic first-passage modeling of single-event burnout in SiC power MOSFETs
authors: Feiyi Liu, Min Guo, Shiyang Chen, Yuhan Jiang, Mingyang Liu, Yang Wang
abstract: Single-event burnout (SEB) in silicon carbide (SiC) power MOSFETs is often characterized by deterministic threshold quantities. Near the boundary between recovery and runaway, stochastic variability can make this threshold description probabilistic rather than sharp. This work introduces a first-passage perspective for stochastic threshold broadening in burnout. The process is described by a reduced electrothermal feedback-relaxation model with an absorbing boundary. The model combines carrier multiplication, avalanche feedback, localized heating, carrier loss, and thermal relaxation. Stochastic carrier and thermal terms represent unresolved event-level variability. The main finding is that finite fluctuations broaden the deterministic burnout threshold into a probabilistic transition band. Noise-induced subthreshold runaway also emerges, where nominally recoverable conditions can still fail through rare stochastic excursions. First-passage-time distributions resolve the time scale of burnout and survival probabilities further distinguish rapid feedback-dominated runaway from delayed stochastic failure. A feedback-relaxation phase diagram organizes recoverable, probabilistic, and rapidly unstable regimes. This framework provides a statistical-physics interpretation of threshold dispersion in single-event burnout of SiC power MOSFETs by linking coarse-grained electrothermal dynamics to probabilistic and time-resolved failure observables.
summary: This paper develops a statistical-physics framework to model the probabilistic failure of SiC power MOSFETs under radiation. It demonstrates that microscopic fluctuations transform a sharp deterministic burnout threshold into a broad transition band and can trigger failure even in nominally safe conditions. This provides a way to bridge the gap between deterministic device simulations and observed experimental variability.
main_problem: Addressing the stochastic nature of Single-Event Burnout (SEB) in SiC power MOSFETs by moving beyond deterministic thresholds to account for threshold broadening and noise-induced subthreshold runaway.
main_result: Finite fluctuations broaden the deterministic burnout threshold into a probabilistic transition band and enable failure under nominally recoverable conditions via rare stochastic excursions.
method: A reduced-order, stochastic electrothermal feedback-relaxation model formulated as a first-passage problem using stochastic differential equations (SDEs) and Euler-Maruyama integration.
model_or_system: A coarse-grained electrothermal model of SiC power MOSFETs subject to heavy-ion-induced ionization, incorporating carrier multiplication, avalanche feedback, Joule heating, and thermal relaxation.
key_observables: Probability of Single-Event Burnout (P_SEB), first-passage-time distributions, survival probability, and threshold dispersion (transition width).
important_parameters: Ionization strength (l), feedback strength (F), thermal relaxation rate (r), noise amplitudes (sigma_n, sigma_Theta), and the thermal indicator (Lambda_th).
main_assumptions: The model uses a coarse-grained approach that does not resolve specific device geometries or spatial profiles of electric fields and current density, and assumes independent noise for carriers and temperature.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows spatial overlap of ion tracks and high-field regions; Figure 2 shows the electrothermal framework; Figures 3-5 illustrate deterministic trajectories, stochastic broadening, and noise-induced subthreshold runaway; Figure 6 details first-passage-time statistics; Figure 7 presents a feedback-relaxation phase diagram.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from a deterministic reduced-order model to a stochastic extension, followed by Monte Carlo trajectory simulations, and concludes with the presentation of a phase diagram and statistical observables.
why_interesting: The paper utilizes a first-passage framework and stochastic differential equations to describe a phase transition between stable and unstable regimes, which is highly relevant to the study of non-equilibrium phase transitions and noise-induced phenomena in open systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Source and field overlap defining the coarse-grained sensitive region Ωs.
Figure 2: Reduced electrothermal framework. The upper solid-line part represents the de- terministic reduced-order model, while the lower dashed-line part represents the stochastic extension.
Figure 3: Deterministic reference trajectories of the reduced electrothermal model. (a) Carrier population n(s). (b) Instantaneous electrothermal source κe(s)n(s). (c) Normal- ized temperature Θ(s).

Figure 4: Stochastic broadening of the burnout transition. (a) Stochastic temperature trajectories near the deterministic boundary. (b) Burnout probability as a function of ionization strength. Error bars denote the binomial standard error. (c) Transition width as a function of noise strength.

Figure 5: Noise-induced subthreshold electrothermal runaway. (a) Stochastic temperature trajectories below the deterministic boundary. (b) Burnout probability as a function of noise strength. Error bars denote the binomial standard error. (c) Comparison between a deterministic recoverable trajectory and a stochastic runaway event.
